skill.name: writing-fragments
skill.description: Runs an interview session that captures rough writing fragments and appends them to one Markdown document for later drafting.
skill.summary: Capture raw writing fragments before shaping an article.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: The static external command findings are false positives from a fenced Markdown example, not executable Ruby or shell code. The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance findings are also false positives with no supporting security-sensitive behavior. The only real concern is user-directed file reading and appending, which is expected for this writing skill and supports publication with a low risk level.

low_findings.title: User-Directed File Read and Append Behavior
low_findings.description: The skill asks the agent to re-read a user-selected Markdown file and append fragments. This is expected functionality, but users should choose the destination path deliberately.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file access instructions are explicit and central to the skill. I found no evidence of hidden paths, overwrites, network transfer, or secret collection.

low_findings.title: False Positive: External Command Detection
low_findings.description: The detected backtick pattern is part of a fenced Markdown document format example. It is not an instruction to execute Ruby, shell, or any external command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are enclosed in a Markdown code fence that shows output formatting. No executable command syntax or tool invocation appears there.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Detection
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography finding points to the frontmatter description. No hash function, cipher, credential handling, or cryptographic operation appears at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is descriptive prose about writing fragments. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, or any cryptographic use in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Detection
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance finding points to asking the user where to save a writing document. This is path selection for user content, not host discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction asks the user for a save path and does not enumerate files, inspect environment details, or probe the operating system.

content.value_statement: Writers often lose strong ideas because they try to outline too early. This skill interviews the user and stores raw fragments in one Markdown file for later article development.
actual_capabilities: Interviews the user to surface claims, vignettes, observations, and rough sentences.
Captures fragments from the first user message instead of waiting for an outline.
Creates a single Markdown document with one working title.
Separates fragments with horizontal rules for easy later review.
Re-reads the target file before appending so user edits are preserved.
Handles user requests to cut, rewrite, or merge specific fragments.
limitations: It does not create outlines, article structures, or final drafts.
It requires a user-approved file path before saving fragments.
It depends on the agent having permission to read and write the chosen file.
It preserves raw material, so fragments may remain incomplete or uneven.

use_cases.title: Develop Essay Raw Material
use_cases.description: Explore an article idea through questions and save promising sentences before deciding on structure.

use_cases.title: Capture Technical Writing Notes
use_cases.description: Collect claims, examples, code scenarios, and complaints for a future technical post.

use_cases.title: Build a Reusable Idea File
use_cases.description: Store separate observations in one Markdown file so they can be mined during later drafting.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Fragment Session
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin with a broad topic and let the skill ask questions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use writing-fragments to help me develop raw material about [topic]. Save the fragments to [path].

prompt_templates.title: Explore a Strong Claim
prompt_templates.scenario: Turn a rough opinion into several usable fragments.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use writing-fragments. I think [claim], but I do not know how to write about it yet. Ask me questions and capture fragments.

prompt_templates.title: Mine an Experience
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert a story or event into article material.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use writing-fragments to interview me about this experience: [event]. Capture scenes, lines, and observations in [path].

prompt_templates.title: Refine an Active Fragment File
prompt_templates.scenario: Continue a session while preserving manual edits.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use writing-fragments with the existing file at [path]. Re-read it first, then help me add, cut, rewrite, and merge fragments.

output_examples.input: I want to write about why simple tools often beat complex platforms.
output_examples.output: A Markdown fragment file with a working title, a sharp opening claim, a short tool comparison vignette, and separate observations divided by horizontal rules.

output_examples.input: Cut the last one and make the previous fragment sharper.
output_examples.output: The most recent fragment is removed, the previous fragment is rewritten with tighter wording, and the conversation continues without changing the rest of the file.

output_examples.input: I have three disconnected thoughts about learning TypeScript.
output_examples.output: Three readable fragments are captured in order, including a claim, a learner scenario, and a sentence that may work in the final article.

best_practices: Choose a dedicated Markdown file before the session starts.
Answer follow-up questions with concrete scenes, claims, and phrases.
Edit the file during the session when a fragment no longer feels useful.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill when you already need a polished final draft.
Do not ask it to create an outline before collecting fragments.
Do not save fragments into a file that contains unrelated important content.

faq.question: Does this skill write the final article?
faq.answer: No. It gathers raw writing material for a future article, essay, post, or newsletter.

faq.question: Why does it use one Markdown file?
faq.answer: One file keeps fragments visible, editable, and easy to mine during later drafting.

faq.question: Can I edit the file during the session?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill instructs the agent to re-read the file before writing so user edits are preserved.

faq.question: Will it organize my ideas into sections?
faq.answer: No. The skill explicitly avoids outlines, phases, tags, and body headings.

faq.question: What counts as a fragment?
faq.answer: A fragment can be a claim, vignette, quote, complaint, half-thought, list, or sharp sentence.

faq.question: Is this compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
